[IMAGE:0]It's a little after midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Dane Rudhyar (birth name Daniel Chennevière), who was both an influential composer in the modernist school and far and away the twentieth century's most influential astrologer, the figure who integrated astrology with depth psychology and launched the person-centered school of astrology that dominated the field in the second half of the twentieth century.

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

comments.content:
I’ve been doing some research on Plutonic cultural themes for a project I’m working on, and found an increase in a certain subset of what I initially thought was part of the Plutonic current from the second half of the 1970s on.

This was not an increase in the full Plutonic current, but in a subset, which, at the risk of dramatically oversimplifying I’ll call nihilism mixed with explosive rage. This appears to show up in several different fields, and in several different countries and cultures as well.

This struck me as odd, and I filed it away as interesting, but I didn’t have any explanation for it. However, now that I’m looking into the period when Pluto was downgraded from planethood, I found a possible explanation. It turns out that NASA*, among others, called Eris the 10th planet upon its discovery. This would seem to suggest that it could have functioned as a very brief temporary planet.

Based on the myths, nihilistic rage would seem to be a good summary of Eris, and given the discovery in the first half of the 2000s, the planet would have started coming into focus at some point in the early 1970s. I haven’t determined the converse Saturn return yet, but it strikes me that Eris started coming into focus at about the same point in time as the increase in what I initially thought was the Plutonic current got underway.

1)Does the line of reasoning I’m using make sense to you, or is there a massive flaw I’m missing?

2)I plan to try to figure out if Eris was a temporary planet. The best method I’ve come up with is to cast as many charts which were active during the period from when it started coming into focus until the present, with an emphasis on charts active while it was a planet, and seeing if Eris offers any extra information. Does this sound like it would work for determining whether Eris was a planet?

3)I’m thinking of looking at ingresses for any country I can find enough information to interpret events in the period (which will likely be several dozen), but also celebrities, and my own chart and those of my friends who are willing to let me try this out. Are there any other types of charts you can think of I could look at?

4)Are there any additional sources of information you can think of I could look into to identify whether Eris was a temporary planet?

5)There is one additional problem, which makes determine the start of the Eridian period a little complicated: it was discovered in 2005 using images taken in 2003. Is the important moment when the images were taken or the moment that a human being realized a planet was there? I would think it would be when someone realizes its there, but the official discovery date is listed as October 21, 2003, since that’s when the images were taken.

A difference of a few years is enough that it may be possible to determine which one it is, but it will likely take quite a bit more work, and so I figure I’ll ask if you happen to know anything about the topic.

*https://www.nasa.gov/vision/universe/solarsystem/newplanet-072905-images.html

If you use the October 21, 2003 date, Saturn was at 13:13 Cancer and the converse Saturn return is August 8, 1974.

If you use the January 8, 2005 "realization" date (which is probably the correct one - Uranus, Neptune, and Pluto might have "appeared" in others' telescopes before official discovery), Saturn was at 24:20 Caner and the converse Saturn return is July 29, 1975.

Either way you are looking at a shadow period spin-up beginning roughly in 1975. Since IIRC Eris was deplanetized at the same time Pluto was, its spin-down shadow period would end at the same time (June 2036).

Eris itself has basically been within 13-22 Aries for the entire period 1974 to now. By 2036 it still won't have moved out of Aries (that's where it has its aphelion, and when its orbit is 558 years to begin with...)

I took a look, and Pluto was discovered using plates from January 1930. The converse Saturn return of these plates is in December 1900, while the converse Saturn return of Pluto's discovery is in January 1901. What's interesting here is that I found evidence of the earliest stirrings of the Plutonic current in that month.

The interesting thing here is that it seems like planets like to start coming into focus with a bang. I can find a fairly major event on the day each planet started coming into focus. July 29, 1975 lacks such an event. August 8, 1974 on the other hand is the day Richard Nixon resigned.

Given that a large part of what I noticed for nihilistic rage was centred on how American politics degenerated, it seems plausible to me that Richard Nixon's resignation and subsequent pardon might have been the first signs of Eris coming into focus.

replies.title: Eris
replies.content: Eris was given its name by its discoverer, the astronomer Mike Brown. Its current designation is 136199 Eris. It's classified as a dwarf planet, the same as Pluto and Ceres, and its demotion from planet status happened at the same time as Pluto and Ceres, as a result of the same IAU definition of planet.

replies.title: Pluto in Libra
replies.content:
Pluto was in Libra from 1971 to 1983. This suggests disintegration and re-making of all kinds of relationships, from marriage and personal ties, to international diplomacy and trade. When change is resisted, Pluto will erupt in rage. Could the examples in the subset of rage you see be variations on the theme of two (or more) sides unable to reconcile their differences? Such as the oil crisis? Also, punk rock is symbolised by Pluto in Libra; a total destruction of existing ideas of what looks good and how to behave in social spaces.
ChristineS (UK)

replies.title: Re: Pluto in Libra
replies.content:
Well, that was part of my initial thinking, but then the trends continued on into the 80s and 90s. This was when I started to raise an eyebrow, because my initial thought was it had to do with Pluto in Libra and Uranus in Scorpio during the 1970s, but it kept going. Granted, Pluto was in Scorpio until 1995, but it should have died down a little bit in 1995, instead of increasing quite a bit.

Additionally, Pluto tends towards the dark, but it has a somewhat odd take on things: Pluto is highly idealistic. Pluto shoves the darkness away, and pretends it's not there. Whereas starting in the 1970s and picking up steam in the 1980s, there was a wallowing in darkness, which seems very un-Plutonic.

comments.title: Amateur curse doll
comments.content:
A friend of mine with a terrible case of Trump Derangement Syndrome made a doll out of some corn discards. The doll is a representation of a well known blonde haired Fox News correspondent. This friend is an atheist who thinks witchcraft is cool, but only in the most frivolous, dress up for Halloween way. She has no interest in studying the occult. Her husband is currently very ill. They are both of middle age. She made the doll during the last full moon and posted photos of it on social media. She thought it was funny that her cat hissed at the doll and wouldn't go near it.

I have said nothing to her about the obvious foolishness of making the doll. What are the repercussions of making a curse object like she has done with the doll? I'm frightened for her husband's health because of the forces a doll like that invites into the house. I do like this friend of mine and it saddened me to see her playing with fire. Of course I said nothing. I'm keeping this anonymous, of course.

Thanks for all you do and thanks for Magic Monday.

replies.title: Re: Amateur curse doll
replies.content: You should be concerned. Magic doesn't care whether you know what you're doing or not. If she was able to focus any significant amount of negative energy into the doll, even if it was just by wallowing in hatred while she made it, that energy is going to manifest itself in her life and the lives of anyone close to her. Exactly how that'll work out depends on the fine details of the curse, of course, but one way or another it won't be pleasant for anyone involved.

replies.title: Re: Amateur curse doll
replies.content:
My hypothesis is that it's a severe case of moral cognitive dissonance. Economic globalization and the tacit encouragement of mass illegal immigration both had the effect of driving down working class wages in the US to Third World levels while leaving salaries from the middle class on up untouched. That was very profitable for business, since labor costs are a huge fraction of business expenses, but the flip side was that it forced tens of million of working class families in the US into poverty and misery. People in the unaffected classes ignored this or loudly insisted it wasn't happening, and chattered on endlessly about the benefits of globalization and the plight of immigrants, as though nobody anywhere was getting the short end of the stick.

Trump's rise to power made it impossible for that state of hypocritical self-deception to continue, because he focused his candidacy on exactly those issues, and then followed through on his promises. That forced the self-proclaimed Good People to face the implications of the policies they supported and benefited from -- and in the cartoonish logic of today's political morality, since they'd done a Bad Thing, that made them Bad People, not Good People. So, rather than face up to the fact that they'd smiled, nodded, and profited from the impoverishment and immiseration of tens of millions of American families, they wigged out. Shrieking "Orange Man Bad! Orange Man Bad!" at the top of their lungs is their last defense against realizing that they're morally no better than the people they despise.

replies.title: Re: Amateur curse doll
replies.content:
I rather agree. One other thing I sometimes think is that the whole Meritocracy ideology that Progress venerates gives the non-Precariat classes yet another pole by which to confirm that said Non-Precariat salary-earners deserves every bit of wealth that comes to it. It's not the Middle Class Salary-earners job to 'lift up' the downtrodden when such people (non-Salary-class/Degreed) won't help themselves with the paths salary class provides for becoming a member of the Good People.

Speaking on a more esoteric level I read a book by Nithyananda recently that had a topic on Mind - specifically on the habitual tendency of many people to criticize. He made the point that the human species isn't smart enough to have 2 Minds at the same time - one that reserves criticism for others but uses a different mind reserved for one's self. Each human only has his/her one single mind. Esoterically, when one falls into the habit of criticism of others - like with TDS (or I suppose Britain's version - BorisDS) one's mind is revealing one thinks these things also of one's self. So...energy-wise (Shakti) calling someone a racist or sexist (or other 'ist') trains one's own mind and subsequently energies to become exactly that..racist, sexist, whatever-ist. And the more often you do it - the more you are self-training to become the thing you criticize (energy-wise that is).

If one wants better physical and mental health a good way to supplement other health habits is by leaving criticism by the wayside for a few minutes a day when something that would otherwise trigger a criticism habit pops up. Then begin extending the length of time of non-criticizing. Each time one overcomes that mind-habit it frees up formerly bound energy that can be redirected in a better way for the future.

Once he started talking about that I have become more careful of my own speech to others because I'd rather train my mind to be more beneficial - both for myself and others also.

Namely that, (a) I'm overall less irritable and annoyed by mundane things/people/happenings around me, (b) I feel like I'm lot more calm, relaxed and patient, and (c) noticing that my mental time seems even more devoted to the metaphysical space than before. 3. And I'm assuming Tarot would be a perfectly suitable divination practice for this setup, yeah? If not, what would be better?

Once again, thank you so much for all of this!!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, if you're practicing the ritual systematically. It gets even better, by the way.

2) Three months is fine. Usually I have people practice daily for a month or two, and then add in the Middle Pillar. As for the centers of the MP, there's a lot of flexibility; the most important points are that you should have a sky deity for the crown sphere (the one above the head), an earth deity for the root sphere (the one at the feet), and a deity of your own gender for the genital center. This would be one option:

Crown Center - Zeus
Throat Center - Hermes
Heart Center - Aphrodite
Genital Center - Dionysos
Root Center - Gaia

But there are plenty of other options, of course.

3) Yes, or any other oracle you prefer. I've done it with four different Tarot decks, geomancy, the Ogham, the Coelbren alphabet, the Lenormand deck, electional astrology, and my forthcoming Sacred Geometry Oracle, among other things.

4) I'll be doing a series of posts on discursive meditation here shortly. In the meantime, here's a post on the subject, and most of my books on magic discuss it as well -- The Druidry Handbook has the most complete guide I've written so far.

comments.title: well dedication ceremony
comments.content:
Hello. This past Saturday was the 2nd anniversary of my bicycle accident, which occurred very close to a roadside well. Given that I received great help from strangers which got me scooped up off the road and taken to hospital by strangers, while I was unconscious and unaware, and that I recovered extremely quickly from my injuries, I had developed a close affinity to this well. I think I told last year, on the first anniversary of my fall, how I had asked its owners for permission to say a prayer at it, and take some water, which I have carefully kept for a year with the intention of using it to dedicate another old well, which is on our farm, to be used in the healing and protective power of the Lady of the Wells, who, in Ireland is also called Bridget or Brid (pronounced Breege).

So, anyway, during the year, my husband and I did some clearing and cleaning, and he repaired and rebuilt its stone surround, and I created a dedication ceremony, built around the SOP. You should know that my regular SOP is built around an Irish pantheon, and during your recent SOP articles, I changed the opening to a three-ray style one. Brid is normally invoked at the third ray of my opening (deity I feel a particular attachment to) and at my south elemental invocation/banishing, because she is the lady of the fire.

However, for this dedication ceremony, I felt moved to create an SOP that includes Brid at every step, invoking her different attributes, and because this felt new, but also felt right, I want to share it here, and invite your comments/consideration.

The SOP I used in the dedication ceremony invoked Brid as follows:
*touch forehead* Brid, Goddess of Inspiration and Poetry
*touch womb centre* Brid, Goddess of Hearth, Childbirth, and Healing
*hands out at heart level* Brid, Goddess of Smithcraft and making
*invoke/banish East* Dawn-born Brid, Goddess of Spring, breath on the wind
*invoke/banish South* Flame-headed Brid, Goddess of Fire and Hearth
*invoke/banish West* Life-giving Brid, Goddess of Wells and waters
*invoke/banish North* Green-mantled Brid, Goddess of Earth and of those who graze her
*invoke Spirit Below* Earth Mother Brid, mother of all
*invoke Spirit Above* Bright Exalted Brid, sun of all
*invoke Spirit Within* Brid, soul-friend, who knows sorrow and brings healing, to dwell within this place and these waters

I also used some separate prayers to invite her ongoing presence into the life and work of this well.

I vibrated her name 19 times during this ceremony. (19 is a number strongly associated with the tradition of keeping her fire lit at Kildare, which has recently been revived after several centuries in abeyance.)

Once I had conceived the thought that the SOP could be constructed entirely around Brid, I found it altogether suspiciously easy to find the attributes that would locate her at all the different positions - like it was not me who guided the choosing of them.

Anyway, the work of dedication is done, now to continue to care for the well, and enjoy the water. When I visited yesterday, and hung out, a little frog appeared and exchanged looks with me for quite a long spell. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: well dedication ceremony
replies.content: Excellent! I doubt you had much to do with choosing the attributes. You might at some point consider sharing the Brid-centered SoP with her other worshipers if you haven't done so already -- they might well appreciate it, and it would likely do a great deal of good for them, for everyone in Ireland, and for the land itself.

I have just heard about this pilgrimage and am minded to join it next year. I'm sure it will be an opportunity for that kind of sharing. http://brigidsway.ie

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
is there a magical effect in a child’s baptism, provided it happens in a sacramental church and the priest knows what he is doing?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes.

Some quotes from C.W. Leadbeater's chapter on Baptism on this topic:

"What help can be given to a soul when he first comes into a new physical body? ... He comes laden with teh result of his past lives, which means that he has within him seeds of good qualities and also seeds of evil qualities."

"The student of the inner life will understand that the development of these qualities depends largely upon teh surroundings given to the child. If he is surrounded with love and gentleness, the love and gentleness in him will be called out and developed. If, on the contrary, he meets with angry vibrations and irritability, if there is in him the least trace of germs of that kind (as there is almost sure to be), they will be called out and developed; and it makes an enormous difference to his life which set of vibrations is first set in motion."

"The sacrament of Baptism is especially designed to deal with this state of affairs. The water used is magnetized with a specifal view to the effect of its vibrations upon teh higher vehicles, so that all the germs of good qualities in the unformed astral and mental bodies of the child may thereby receive a strong stimulus, while at the same time the germs of evil may be isolated and deadened."

"It is a consecration and a setting apart of the new set of vehicles to the true expression of the soul within, and to the service of the Great White Brotherhood; yet it also has its hidden side with regard to these vehicles themselves, and when the ceremony is properly and intelligently performed there can be no doubt that its effect is a powerful one. It is distinctly, therefore, what may be called an act of white magic, producing definite results which affect the whole future life of the child."

...

So that's what happens when a baptism is done well, by people who know what they are doing.

I'd suggest that the baptism has an effect no matter what, though-- and imagine what results, then, when it's performed badly or haphazardly, in the kind of church that drips with the rage that comes from sexual repression.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. All the Christian sacraments are potent magical ceremonies; they were adapted from older Pagan Mystery traditions by people who knew exactly what they were doing, at a time when the methods of magic were much better understood than they are today.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This gave me a chuckle. My mother is Protestant, and recently attended the (Orthodox) baptism of our newest addition to the family. It was the first Orthodox baptism she'd ever seen-- at her church they can just pat you on the head with a wet hand and call you baptized. I thought she might fall off her chair seeing how *complicated* we make it. It looks very Pagan to her... ;)

It's no great wonder that the church has such rituals. Everyone did, in the ancient world. The curious thing is how/why so many Protestants (and after them, the non-religious) ditched them. They seem to address a universal human need-- and if that is the case, what are the consequences for people who go without?

Maybe it's something like malnutrition.

comments.title: Geomancy casting for newborn
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

I cast a geomancy chart for a newborn girl and got these figures:

1st Mother (1st House) – Amissio
2nd Mother (2nd House) – Laetitia
3rd Mother (3rd House) – Albus
4th Mother (4th House) – Tristitia

1st Daughter (5th House) – Fortuna Minor
2nd Daughter (6th House) – Populus
3rd Daughter (7th House) – Amissio
4th Daughter ( 8th House) – Tristitia

1st Niece (9th House) – Albus
2nd Niece (10th House) – Fortuna Major
3rd Niece (11th House) – Fortuna Minor
4th Niece (12th House) – Puella

Right Witness – Tristitia
Left Witness – Caput Draconis
Judge – Conjunctio
Reconciler – Fortuna Minor

My questions are:

1) How would you read the figures in each house?
2) Amissio (1st and 7th houses): Does it mean she and her spouse will lose their hearts to each other?
3) Albus (3rd and 9th houses): Peace, quiet progress, use of intelligence (according to your book). I understand there is great potential for education but Albus is strong in 3rd house and weak in 9th house... Any comments?
4) Tristitia (4th and 8th houses): Father’s influence and spouse’s economic standing ruled by a figure which means lowered expectations though it also means building, stability, grounding and keeping secrets. How would you read it?
5) Fortuna Minor (5th and 11th houses): Obviously seems to link her passions to her friends but what does it mean a strong Fortuna Minor in 5th versus a weak Fortuna Minor in 11th?
6) Does the Judge or Witnesses, Reconciler provide any additional information within the context of the casting?

Thank you and have a nice day.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy casting for newborn
replies.content: A professional geomancer would normally charge, say, $100-200 for the answers to your questions, which amount to a complete delineation of the chart, and would take a couple of hours of careful analysis. That's more than I'm willing to do for free on a comment thread that already has more than 130 comments on it! Since the child's just been born -- congrats to the parents, btw -- you have the time to study geomancy intensively, cast plenty of other readings, learn how to do it, and return to this chart with the necessary background. I suggest you get to work...

replies.title: Re: Geomancy casting for newborn
replies.content:
OK, fair enough.

Could you then just comment on Albus in houses 3rd (strong) and 9th (weak)
and Fortuna Minor in houses 5th (strong) and 11th (weak) ?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy casting for newborn
replies.content: The strong and weak qualities of figures in houses are not equivalent to good and bad; they simply mean that, say, Albus in the 9th is going to have a stronger effect on the chart as a whole than in the 3rd -- so 9th house influences such as religion and higher education will have a stronger influence on her life than 3rd house influences such as siblings and neighbors. The same sort of thing applies whenever you have strong and weak figures, as in the 5th and 11th -- and they're only one part of the mix. (For example, having a mobile figure shared in 5th and 11th also means that she'll tend to move from friendship to friendship and from love affair to love affair, rather than having a few steady friends or a single relationship -- until she finally meets the right person, and then that's all she wrote. Don't overstress any one indicator in a chart!

comments.title: Esoteric Effects of Caffeine
comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Effects of Caffeine
replies.content:
I'm also interested in this, given that caffeine is clearly the main drug of choice in our society at the moment.

I also wanted to add to the comment someone made last week about how psychedelics were a part of helping them break through the rationalist, materialist worldview they were raised with. This has also been my experience. They've helped me with healing some addictive and compulsive behaviours like porn and over-eating, and I'd say they're part of the reason why I'm here.

I was interested to read of Dion Fortune's take on psychedelics as destabilizing the various subtle bodies in a way that may be hard to put back together, and take that as a cautionary reminder, which certainly rings true when I think of people who use large quantities of LSD, mushrooms, ayahuasca...etc over the course of years, especially when divorced from any kind of ritual, or meditation or other kind of grounding spiritual practice. I recall reading about a study which compared people's subjective reports of the impacts psychedelics had had on them, and that the people who reported most positive impacts were those who combined psychedelic use with a regular discipline of meditation.

I have my own qualms about the current trendiness of psychedelics among certain subsets of society (especially driven by the Silicon valley scene) and the hyped-up claims of their potential but I suspect that, like alcohol and caffeine and some other drugs, they can have a place when used judiciously in moderate amounts on an occasional basis as part of an overall balanced lifestyle that includes regular spiritual practice (rather than as a substitute for time-tested practices!). Maybe in an ideal world they would be redundant, but I think that for many of us, we need a glimpse of the possibilities in order to even try meditation, prayer and other spiritual or magical practices. After all, if you believe that we live in a dead material universe, and everyone and everything around you reinforces that perspective, why bother?

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Effects of Caffeine
replies.content:
I have no personal experience with coffee, as it gives me migraines. I know that copious amounts of green tea, my beverage of choice, seems to have no negative results -- in fact, there's some evidence that it's good for meditation, as Zen monks are famous for guzzling green tea!

I've encountered claims that combining coffee and yoga will mess you over, but I don't know whether those are true or not. Certainly most occultists in the Western world for the last couple of centuries drank modest amounts of coffee as a matter of course, and seem not to have been harmed by it.

Thank you for your words about the Red Hook event! It was a great time.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Effects of Caffeine
replies.content:
Green tea has quite a bit of L-theanine. My personal experience on adding about 200mg into my morning cup of coffee has been very positive. Normally, coffee makes me a bit twitchy. Not enough to bother me, but it was definitely noticeable when meditating. With the addition, the stimulant effect is at least as strong, but much smoother. My consumption also dropped on its own to two cups per day, half of what it used to be.

Might be worth trying for coffee aficionados.

comments.title: Kundalini awakening and overcharged nervous system
comments.content: I am a ceremonial magician. About a year and a half ago, I began to have stirrings of kundalini. Initially these were in the form of feelings of erotic ecstasy, intense sensations of energy concentrating at the base of the spine and then  moving up, intense heat in the body and sweating, occasional visionary experiences and involuntary muscle spasms throughout my body. Over the time since, most of these (enjoyable) aspects have petered out, but I still have periods during the night when I get the muscle spasms and generally feel wound up and overstimulated without an obvious cause. Apart from the nuisance of losing sleep, am I at risk of the more serious consequences of kundalini awakening like burning out my nervous system? What can do I do to restore harmony and get better rest?

replies.title: Re: Kundalini awakening and overcharged nervous system
replies.content: Can you handle a diet fairly high in meat? That will help settle your nervous system and reconnect you to your physical body, which is after all made of meat. Regular massage is also very useful, and you may want to avoid any breathing exercises more complex than ordinary rhythmic breathing, so as not to stir things up. Your body basically needs to regroup and recover from the experience; treat it as though you've been ill and need to restore your vital energy, and you'll be fine.

replies.title: Re: House of health in non-medical geomancy

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Knee surgery follow-up
replies.content:
Very often formulating an intention is all you need to do! Magical methods are useful when you need something more than that. Yes, you should probably pick a different topic.

If your rituals sound like prayers you're doing it right. "May I receive..." is meant to be impersonal, so it can be done by people who don't have any relationship to deities at all. Personal taste is a valid guide here.

As for getting something for nothing, not at all. Your therapist will be paid, right? You're just looking for an opportunity to have a mutually productive interaction with another therapist.

replies.title: Re: Knee surgery follow-up

You're most welcome and thank you.

Banishing phases need to be handled with some care. Very often, for healing purposes, it's best to focus on banishing pain, discomfort, and debility, and leave it at that.

comments.title: improving visualizations

Tim

replies.title: Re: improving visualizations
replies.content: Put aside some time every day to practice visualizing as such. Tomorrow, for example, spend five minutes or so imagining a small blue pyramid as clearly as you can. Imagine yourself picking it up, turning it over, feeling the weight and bulk and texture of the thing. Do that every other day. On alternate days, in your imagination, walk along some familiar stretch of street or trail. Do this repeatedly, and now and then walk down the actual street or trail and see how much you missed. Like muscles, the mind gains strength from exercise.

3) And I know I’ve brought this up before and I know it’s probably irrational but the thought won’t go away for some reason: ... is possible for someone to put in lifetime after lifetime of diligent spiritual work only to have some future incarnation “turn to the dark side” in some drastic way, do a 180 and start setting down a path that leads to this? If you've gotten solidly on the Path and then try to back away from it, you get socked in the gut by the consequences of your own stupidity, harder and harder, until the lesson sinks in -- and since you've gotten solidly on the Path, you no longer have the kind of blithe ignorance that enables you to pretend to yourself that that's not what's happening. The people who end up negating themselves, according to tradition, are the ones who take up magic in a corrupted form and stick with it even though they encounter opportunity after opportunity to do something less foredoomed. A spiritual bath of hyssop and rue, brewed before dawn.

2. Considering my attacker is determined to ruin their life by persistently attacking me, I was looking for a more long term solution. I found the description of the Mirror Box spell on Lucky Mojo's site. Is it possible to perform this kind of spell without it being malevolent? replies.title: Re: Uncrossing workings and more aggressive types of protection workings

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
These days I'm a 32nd degree Mason in both the Northern and Southern jurisdictions of the Scottish Rite -- the rituals are very different. (The Northern jurisdiction is the states north of the Mason-Dixon line and east of the Mississippi; everything else is Southern. Yes, that means Alaska is Southern.) Some Scottish Rite bodies are full of occultists, some aren't -- it really varies from place to place.

One thing you may not know is that you can't join the Scottish Rite until you're a Master Mason in good standing. Your ordinary Masonic lodge -- "blue lodge" in Mason-speak -- is where you get your first three degrees. Ask around, see if you can meet with some lodge members and find a lodge that has younger members, and then ask for a petition for membership; it's worth doing for a lot of reasons, and access to local occult circles is often one of them.

replies.title: Re: Some disjointed questions
replies.content:
The Christian Science term for it is "Malicious Animal Magnetism," commonly abbreviated by therm as "M.A.M." Mrs. Eddy had quite a lot to say in print about its power, and probably talked about it more in private with her favored pupils than she gave out to the public in print.

One of the very last actual trials under the old Massachusetts laws against witchcraft happened in 1878 in Ipswich, where one of Mrs. Eddy's pupils brought a charge of witchcraft against another of her pupils for using M.A.M. against him. (The judge threw the case out of court as ridiculous.) Mrs. Eddy herself attended the trial as an observer.

To the best of my knowledge, M.A.M. is still an active concern among Christian Scientists.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The term "spiritual development," at the human stage of evolution, is a bit of a misnomer, since we don't have anything to develop on the spiritual plane yet. Our job is to clear way any problems with our astral bodies -- emotional maturity is part of that, since the astral plane is the plane of emotion -- and then to work on building a mental body, all the while figuring out how to cooperate with the various beneficial spiritual influences and energies that are around us.

In effect, "spiritual development" means working on whatever you need to work on to get to the point when you can begin to build a body on the lowest of the spiritual planes. If that's emotional maturity right now, that's what it means.

comments.title: Anger
comments.content:
I'm a longtime reader of your various blogs, and a magical practitioner, though a fairly new one.

I've a question about anger. For most of my adult life I have been angry. I've used it productively, for the most part: it is channelled into my writing, into (in the past) forms of political activism, into 'changing the world', supposedly for the better.

But I've long realised that the anger is not something which is created by 'injustice' or the like (the reason mst activists give themslves for their own levels of anger and intolerance) but is a flame which has burned within me for as long as I can remember, and which can be used to burn for good or ill. As I say, my own flame does not burn those around me, mostly. But it does endlessly burn, and I find myself looking all the time for reasons to nourish it. There are plenty to be found, of course, in just a few quick clicks on the internet.

My question is: is there a regular personal working or exercise which can help control, banish or regulate anger? Some days it feels quite overwhelming, and leads to depressive thoughts and feelings. Any advice would be gratefully received.

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content: It sounds to me as though this is something you've carried over from a previous life. In that case, figuring out how to deal with it may be the central task you face in this life. There are no simple solutions for something that large and complex, but daily meditation is the basic working tool for dealing with unresolved emotional issues. You don't have to meditate on the anger; you simply need to build the capacity to deal with things on the mental plane of impersonal ideas, rather than the astral plane of personal feelings, and that will enable you to untangle the underlying energy from its forms and put it to more productive work.

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content: I was born angry, and as a younger child I reveled in my anger.  Once,. when I was about 8, my mother said, "Robby, if you don't get control of that anger of yours, someone is going to kill you!"  And her tone of voice suggested to me that it might be she herself who would be that unnamed "someone."  So I began to try to get control of it.  My first efforts were to try to back away from the thing that was the pretext for my anger.  Eventually I figured out that my anger was a fire that craved fuel, and that it was willing to manipulate me to give it that fuel.  So I tried refusing its demands, and after quite a number of long months I began to get better at dialing it back.  By the time I was in high school, I had pretty well starved my anger to death.  But it was a long hard struggle, maybe eight or ten years of wrestling with it.  --  Maybe this can be useful to others.  I was very very young when I started working on my own anger.  If I had been older, I might have been able to go about it more efficiently.
